source.title: Distinct roles for direct and indirect pathway striatal neurons in reinforcement.

confidence_rationale: Directly tests the approach/avoidance asymmetry at the reinforcement learning level: optogenetic stimulation of D1 MSNs (direct/Go pathway) induced persistent positive reinforcement; stimulation of D2 MSNs (indirect/NoGo pathway) induced transient punishment/suppression. These are qualitatively distinct outcomes, not merely different magnitudes of the same signal. This is the key evidence that Go and NoGo sub-channels carry non-exchangeable information -- exactly what ARC-030 requires.
failure_signatures: If Go and NoGo were symmetric, both D1 and D2 stimulation would produce equivalent learning effects of opposite sign. Instead, D1 produced persistent reinforcement while D2 produced only transient punishment -- a qualitative asymmetry.
If a pure NoGo architecture were sufficient, D2 stimulation alone would support normal learning; it produced only transient punishment without persistent reinforcement.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Stimulating D1 receptor-expressing neurons induced persistent reinforcement, whereas stimulating D2 receptor-expressing neurons induced transient punishment, indicating that activation of these circuits is sufficient to modify the probability of performing future actions.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-030 requires symmetric Go (approach) and NoGo (avoidance) sub-channels because they carry distinct information: Go drives persistent approach learning while NoGo drives avoidance/punishment signaling. The qualitative asymmetry (persistent reinforcement vs. transient punishment) matches the architectural requirement that the two channels cannot be collapsed into one. An architecture with only D2/NoGo pathways would lose the persistent approach-learning signal entirely.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study measures self-stimulation reinforcement, not naturalistic approach/avoidance in a cost-benefit environment. The 'transient' vs. 'persistent' distinction may partly reflect stimulation protocol differences. However, the qualitative dissociation in learning timescale is robust.
mapping.source_context: Used optogenetic self-stimulation in dorsomedial striatum; D1-Cre and D2-Cre mice; within-session and across-session comparisons of reinforcement magnitude and persistence.

confidence_components.notes: Nature Neuroscience letter, same group as Kravitz 2010, more focused on reinforcement than motor control. Mapping fidelity high because the finding is directly about approach vs. avoidance learning signals.
